# Rmd Workflow: Conversions and Tables {#sec-rmd-workflow}

::: {.callout-note}
## Sources

Blog posts `17-rapidconversionRtoRmd`,
`38-tableplacementrmarkdown`, `07-multilanguagequartodemo`.
:::

## Prerequisites

Answer the following questions to see if you can bypass this
chapter. You can find the answers at the end of the chapter in
@sec-quiz-answers-12.

1. What is the quickest way to convert an existing `.R`
   script into an `.Rmd` or `.qmd`, preserving comments as
   prose?
2. Why do tables and figures in rendered PDFs often appear in
   unexpected places, and what do `fig.pos` and `tbl-pos` do?
3. How do you ensure that a figure or table label defined in
   an Rmd is clickable (hyperlinked) in both the HTML and the
   PDF outputs?

## Learning objectives

By the end of this chapter you should be able to:

- Convert between `.R`, `.Rmd`, and `.qmd` quickly and
  losslessly.
- Control LaTeX float placement with `fig.pos`/`tbl-pos` and
  inline anchoring (`[H]`).
- Produce cross-referenced figures and tables that hyperlink
  correctly in HTML, PDF, and Word outputs.
- Switch a project's engine between `knitr` and Quarto without
  rewriting chunks.
- Run a multi-language document (R, Python, Julia) in a single
  render.

## Orientation

A mature Rmd workflow is the difference between an analysis
that can be quickly iterated on and one that is a maze of
manual edits. This chapter collects the practical tricks that
pay off on the fifth rendered version of a document.

## Converting between formats

TODO: `knitr::spin()` for `.R` → `.Rmd`; `knitr::purl()` for
`.Rmd` → `.R`; `quarto convert` for `.ipynb` ↔ `.qmd`.

## Float placement in PDF

TODO: why LaTeX floats rearrange; `fig.pos = 'H'`; the
`float` package; `tbl-pos: 'H'` in Quarto.

## Cross-references

TODO: `{#fig-scatter}` / `{#tbl-demog}` and their `@fig-`
companions. When HTML and PDF disagree.

## Engine choice: knitr vs Quarto

TODO: differences in chunk options; options that work in both;
migration strategies.

## Multi-language documents

TODO: a `.qmd` with R, Python, and Julia chunks, all talking
to each other via `reticulate`.

## With AI assistance {.unnumbered}

::: {.callout-tip}
## Prompts to try

1. Paste a `.R` script and ask the LLM to convert it to a
   `.qmd` with appropriate chunk options. Compare to
   `knitr::spin()`'s output. Does the LLM preserve all
   comments as prose or silently drop some?
2. Paste a PDF showing a misplaced figure and ask the LLM to
   diagnose. Does it propose `fig.pos = 'H'`? If yes, does
   it also warn that `H` requires `\usepackage{float}`?
3. Ask the LLM to write a minimal multi-engine `.qmd` that
   runs an R chunk, passes a value to Python via
   `reticulate`, and prints the result. Verify the render
   succeeds.
:::

## Exercises

1. Take a recent `.R` analysis and convert it to a `.qmd`
   via `knitr::spin`. Re-render. Note what `spin` handles
   well and what needs manual cleanup.
2. In a Quarto PDF, force every table to appear exactly
   where declared in the source (no LaTeX float
   rearrangement). Verify with a three-table document.
3. Build a minimal reproducible example (MWE) of a
   cross-reference that works in HTML but not in PDF. File
   the bug upstream or post it on the Quarto discussion
   forum.

## Quiz answers {#sec-quiz-answers-12}

1. `knitr::spin('script.R')` is the canonical fast path: it
   turns a commented `.R` file into a rendered `.Rmd` by
   treating any comment starting with `#'` as prose and
   everything else as code chunks. For `.qmd`, the same
   convention works under `quarto convert`.
2. LaTeX treats figures and tables as floats and rearranges
   them by default to avoid bad page breaks. `fig.pos = 'H'`
   (requires the `float` package) and `tbl-pos: 'H'` in
   Quarto YAML tell LaTeX 'place this exactly here, no
   reflow'. In chunks, `fig.pos = 'htbp'` means 'try here,
   then top, then bottom, then a float page' in order.
3. Use Quarto's cross-reference system:
   `{#fig-scatter}` on the figure plus `@fig-scatter` in the
   prose. Quarto handles HTML and PDF (and Word) anchoring
   with the same source syntax. Hand-rolled `\label{}` /
   `\ref{}` works only in PDF.
